annotations.label: Combinatory logic

anchor.snippet: Combinatory logic is a notation to eliminate the need for quantified variables in mathematical logic

annotations.note: Mathlib has no formalization of combinatory logic; the `Mathlib/Control/Combinators.lean` file is about monad combinators in the Haskell sense, unrelated to CL.

annotations.label: Combinator

anchor.snippet: A combinator is a higher-order function that uses only function application and earlier defined combinators to define a result from its arguments

annotations.note: No formal definition of a combinator (higher-order function with no free variables) is present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Lambda-term

anchor.snippet: Lambda calculus is concerned with objects called lambda-terms

annotations.note: Mathlib does not formalize the syntax of lambda calculus (lambda terms).

annotations.label: Abstraction (lambda)

anchor.snippet: are called abstractions

annotations.note: Lambda abstraction as an object-language construct is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Application (lambda)

anchor.snippet: are called applications

annotations.note: Lambda application as a syntactic construct of an object lambda calculus is not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Normal form (lambda)

anchor.snippet: then it cannot be reduced, and is said to be in normal form

annotations.note: There is no formal notion of beta-normal form for lambda terms in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Square function example

anchor.snippet: consider the function that computes the square of a number

annotations.note: This is an illustrative lambda-calculus example, not a theorem; no corresponding artifact in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Church–Turing thesis

anchor.snippet: According to the Church–Turing thesis , both models can express any possible computation

annotations.note: Grep for `Church.Turing` in Mathlib yields no hits; the thesis is informal and not stated in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Combinatory term

anchor.snippet: A combinatory term has one of the following forms

annotations.note: Combinatory terms as an inductive syntactic type are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Primitive combinators

anchor.snippet: The primitive functions are combinators , or functions that, when seen as lambda terms, contain no free variables

annotations.note: No notion of primitive combinator is formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Reduction rule for combinators

anchor.snippet: each primitive combinator comes with a reduction rule of the form

annotations.note: Mathlib does not define a reduction relation on combinatory terms.

annotations.label: Identity combinator I

anchor.snippet: the identity combinator

annotations.note: The CL identity combinator I (with its reduction rule Ix = x) is not formalized; the meta-level `id` function in core Lean is unrelated.

annotations.label: K combinator

anchor.snippet: which manufactures constant functions

annotations.note: The K combinator of combinatory logic is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: S combinator

anchor.snippet: A third combinator is

annotations.note: The S combinator of combinatory logic is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Extensional vs intensional equality

anchor.snippet: We say the terms are extensionally equal

annotations.note: No formal extensional-equality relation on combinatory or lambda terms exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Fixed point combinator

anchor.snippet: A more interesting combinator is the fixed point combinator

annotations.note: Mathlib has fixed-point theorems on order-theoretic structures but no fixed-point combinator of CL/lambda calculus.

annotations.label: Completeness of S-K basis

anchor.snippet: S and K can be composed to produce combinators that are extensionally equal to any lambda term

annotations.note: The completeness of the {S,K} basis for lambda calculus is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: T[] transformation

anchor.snippet: may be defined as follows

annotations.note: The T[] translation from lambda terms into SK combinators is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Abstraction elimination

anchor.snippet: This process is also known as abstraction elimination

annotations.note: Abstraction elimination is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Bracket abstraction

anchor.snippet: It is related to the process of bracket abstraction

annotations.note: Bracket abstraction is not defined in Mathlib.

anchor.snippet: we will convert the lambda term

annotations.note: This worked example of the T[] translation is not present in Mathlib.

anchor.snippet: the T [ ] construction may expand a lambda term of length n to a combinatorial term of length

annotations.note: The O(n^3) blowup bound for the T[] translation is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: η-reduction rule

anchor.snippet: if we take into account the η-reduction rule

annotations.note: η-reduction on lambda or combinatory terms is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.note: One-point combinator bases are not defined in Mathlib.

anchor.snippet: The simplest known one-point basis is a slight modification of

annotations.note: No formalization of one-point bases in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Infinitely many one-point bases

anchor.snippet: there exist infinitely many such bases

annotations.note: This existence statement about one-point CL bases is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: B and C combinators

anchor.snippet: included two combinators which are now called

annotations.note: The B and C combinators of CL are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: B, C expressed via S and K

anchor.snippet: how they in turn can be expressed using only

annotations.note: The SK encodings of B and C are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: CL_I calculus

anchor.snippet: A distinction must be made between the CL K as described in this article and the CL I calculus

annotations.note: The CL_I subsystem is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Reverse conversion L[]

anchor.snippet: The conversion L [ ] from combinatorial terms to lambda terms is trivial

annotations.note: The L[] embedding from CL into lambda calculus is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Normal form (combinatory)

anchor.snippet: A normal form is any combinatory term in which the primitive combinators that occur, if any, are not applied to enough arguments to be simplified

annotations.note: Normal forms for combinatory terms are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Undecidability of normal form

anchor.snippet: It is undecidable whether a general combinatory term has a normal form

annotations.note: Mathlib has Rice's theorem for partial recursive functions (`Computability.rice` in `Mathlib/Computability/Halting.lean`), but no statement of undecidability specific to combinatory-logic normal forms.

annotations.label: Predicate combinator

anchor.snippet: let a predicate be a combinator that, when applied, returns either

annotations.note: The notion of a CL predicate combinator is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Nontrivial predicate

anchor.snippet: is nontrivial if there are two arguments

annotations.note: Nontriviality for CL predicates is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Complete combinator

anchor.snippet: is complete if

annotations.note: Completeness for CL predicate combinators is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Analogue of Rice's theorem

anchor.snippet: An analogue of Rice's theorem for this toy model then says that every complete predicate is trivial

annotations.note: The classical Rice's theorem is in `Mathlib/Computability/Halting.lean` as `Computability.rice`, but its CL analogue is not stated.

annotations.label: No predicate for normal form

anchor.snippet: there is no complete predicate that can discriminate between terms that have a normal form and terms that do not have a normal form

annotations.note: This CL-specific corollary of the Rice analogue is not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: No EQUAL predicate

anchor.snippet: there is no complete predicate, say EQUAL

annotations.note: Undefinability of a CL EQUAL predicate is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Curry–Howard correspondence

anchor.snippet: The Curry–Howard isomorphism implies a connection between logic and programming

annotations.note: Grep for `Curry.Howard` returns no Mathlib files; the propositions-as-types correspondence is not formalized as a Mathlib theorem.

annotations.label: Axioms AK and AS

anchor.snippet: The K and S combinators correspond to the axioms

annotations.note: The Hilbert-style axiom schemes AK and AS for implicational logic are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Modus ponens rule

anchor.snippet: function application corresponds to the detachment

annotations.note: Modus ponens as a syntactic deduction rule of a Hilbert-style proof system is not formalized in Mathlib (only meta-level `modus_ponens`-style hints in `Tactic/Explode.lean`).

annotations.label: Completeness for implicational intuitionistic logic

anchor.snippet: The calculus consisting of AK , AS , and MP is complete for the implicational fragment of the intuitionistic logic

annotations.note: Mathlib does not formalize the implicational fragment of intuitionistic logic or its completeness theorem.
